claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 18 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 18 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you a few questions about your child (or children).

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because the age determines the eligibility path:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over typically don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children you're asking about, please let me know all their ages.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - Alex, who's 18 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 18 years old.

Since Alex is 18 (which falls in the 16-19 age range), they may still qualify for Child Benefit if they meet certain education or training requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to understand Alex's current education or training status:

**Is Alex currently:**
1. **In approved education or training** (such as school, college, or an approved training program), OR
2. **Recently left education or training** (within the last few months), OR
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and hasn't been recently?

Please let me know which situation applies to Alex.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is still in education - he's studying for his A-levels at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex is studying for his A-levels at college, he's in approved education. Now I need to check that the education level meets the requirements for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! A-levels are considered approved (non-advanced) education for Child Benefit purposes. Now I need to check a few more details about Alex's education:

**Questions about Alex's A-level studies:**

1. **Is this full-time education?** (At least 12 hours per week of supervised study - this includes time in class, lectures, and supervised coursework)

2. **Is Alex's course funded by an employer**, or has an employer agreed to employ him after the course?

3. **Is Alex married or in a civil partnership?** (This only matters if he is and is not financially dependent on you)

Please let me know the answers to these questions.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, it's full-time - he's at college during the week with regular classes and supervised study time.

No, it's not funded by an employer or anything like that - it's just regular college.

And no, Alex isn't married.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's very helpful. Let me move forward based on this information.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Alex's A-level course meets the education requirements. Now I need to check his work situation:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

For young people aged 16-19 in education, they must work less than 24 hours per week to qualify for Child Benefit. If they work 24 hours or more per week, they're considered to be working full-time and wouldn't qualify.

Does Alex work less than 24 hours per week, or is he not working at all?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - he's just focused on his studies at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Since Alex isn't working, that means he's well under the 24-hour limit. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check about benefits:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, I'm asking about benefits like:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Tax Credits

If Alex is NOT receiving any of these benefits in his own right, that's fine and we can continue. Is Alex receiving any of these benefits?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about apprenticeships:

**Is Alex in an apprenticeship program?**

You mentioned he's doing A-levels at college, which sounds like a regular education course rather than an apprenticeship. Just to confirm - is Alex doing:
- A regular A-level course (not an apprenticeship), OR
- Any type of apprenticeship program?

Please confirm that it's just a regular A-level course at college.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - it's just a regular A-level course at college, not an apprenticeship or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your residency status, as this is a requirement for claiming Child Benefit.

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

If you normally live in the UK, that satisfies the residency requirement. 

Alternatively, if you don't normally live in the UK, there are special circumstances that may qualify you (such as being a Crown servant abroad, having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, etc.).

Please let me know - do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That meets the residency requirement. Now let me check the next criterion.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to understand your relationship with Alex and whether you're responsible for him.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

This means you either:
1. **Live with Alex**, OR
2. **Contribute to his upkeep** (paying at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards his care - things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.), OR
3. **Pay maintenance** for him under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex lives with you, that clearly establishes you're responsible for him. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else who might be claiming

If someone else is claiming, only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child at a time. 

Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or are you the only person claiming (or intending to claim)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check Alex's living situation to see if any special rules apply.

**Is Alex currently:**
1. **In hospital** (for an extended stay)?
2. **In local authority care** (looked after by the council)?
3. **Living with foster parents** (in a fostering arrangement)?
4. **Living normally at home** with you?

From what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you while attending college. Is that correct, or is there a different living situation I should know about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - Alex is just living normally at home with me while he's at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Just one final check:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the local authority (council) providing any financial support for Alex's living costs or upkeep?

Since Alex lives at home with you and you're supporting him, I'm assuming the answer is no - but I need to confirm this to complete the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Based on all the information you've provided, let me reach the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

Based on all the information you've provided, **you meet all the eligibility requirements** for Child Benefit for Alex.

### Why You're Eligible:

✅ **Alex qualifies as a qualifying young person** because:
- He's 18 years old (within the 16-19 age range)
- He's studying full-time A-levels, which is approved non-advanced education
- He's not working (well under the 24-hour per week limit)
- He's not receiving benefits in his own right
- He's not in a disqualifying apprenticeship

✅ **You meet the claimant requirements** because:
- You normally live in the UK
- Alex lives at home with you (establishing responsibility)
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate

### Important to Know:

⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through the tax system. However, you can still claim it to protect your National Insurance credits.

💷 **Payment**: Child Benefit is usually paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits).

📋 **More information and how to apply**: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and studying full-time A-levels at college, which qualifies as approved non-advanced education. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and is not in an apprenticeship. You live in the UK, Alex lives at home with you (establishing responsibility), no one else is claiming for him, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. All Child Benefit eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for their 18-year-old son Alex, who is in approved full-time education (A-levels) and living at home.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 18 years old and studying full-time A-levels at college, which qualifies as approved non-advanced education. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and is not in an apprenticeship. You live in the UK, Alex lives at home with you (establishing responsibility), no one else is claiming for him, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. All Child Benefit eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for their 18-year-old son Alex, who is in approved full-time education (A-levels) and living at home.
